Facts.text: Jews revolt against Rome, capturing fortress of Antonia in Jerusalem
Roman Senate accepts Emperor Galba
St Silverius begins his reign as Catholic Pope
Vikings in longships from modern-day Norway plunder St. Cuthbert's monastery on Lindisfarne Island off the northeast coast of England, capturing and killing monks
Louis V is crowned King of West Francia, co-reigning with his father Lothair until 986
Holy Roman Emperor Frederick III's left leg is amputated at his court in Linz, the most famous record of surgery in the Middle Ages - he survives until August 19
Earthquake strikes Peru
England parliament bans Christmas, Easter and Whitsun festivities, services and celebrations, including in the home, with fines for non-compliance - proves unpopular
Battle at Amegical: English & Portuguese fleet defeats the Spanish
Siddi general Yadi Sakat, razes the Mazagon Fort in Mumbai
English troops attack Brest (300 killed)
Spanish flagship galleon San José is sunk during a battle just off the coast of  Cartagena, Columbia carrying a cargo of gold, silver and emerald valued today in billions
British fleet occupies Belle Île off the Brittany Coast
Laki volcano in southern Iceland begins an 8-month eruption, killing 10,000 people and causing widespread famine throughout Europe and Asia
Commercially made ice cream is first advertised by Mr. Hall in NYC
William Hyde Wollaston invents the first reflective goniometer
39 German states unite under Act of Confederation
An uprising involving three hundred workers in the English town of Pentrich begins but is quashed almost immediately
Washing machine patented by Noah Cushing of Quebec
First UK municipal swimming pool outside of London opens in Liverpool
HMS Beagle sails from Port Famine to Cape Turn
Battle at Gwanga: British troops beat Bantu
Pitcairn Islanders arrive on Norfolk Island
American Civil War: Tennessee votes to secede from the Union
US Sanitary Commission is given executive approval
Valley Campaign-Battle of Cross Keys, Virginia
Ives W. McGaffey of Chicago patents the first vacuum cleaner, calling it a "sweeping machine"
US Congress endorses penny post card
First Home Rule Bill for Ireland defeated by 343 votes to 313 in the British House of Common
Herman Hollerith receives a patent for his punch card calculator
US shoemaker Homer A. Plessy refuses to go to in a segregated RR car (US Supreme Court Plessy v Ferguson)
First car thief; Baron de Zuylen’s Peugeot is stolen by his mechanic in Paris
Belgium government of Schollaert falls
Carl Laemmle incorporates Universal Pictures
34.7°F (1.5°C) in De Bilt, Netherlands
92°F (33.3°C) in De Bilt, Netherlands
Battle of Verdun: Germans capture Fort Vaux after several days of intense fighting
Walt Disney graduates from Benton High School
Nova Aquilae is discovered, the brightest nova since Kepler's nova of 1604
Reds' Edd Roush falls asleep in center during long infield argument Heinie Groh goes to wake him, but umpire ejects Roush for delay of game
1st US-to-Australia flight lands (Sir Charles Kingford)
Venezuelan rebel Rafael Urbina overthrows Fort Amsterdam, Curacao kidnap governor Fruytier
Suriname Work Committee under Louis Doedel forms in Paramaribo
"Carmina Burana", the first work of Carl Orff's Trionfi cantata trilogy, premieres at the Oper Frankfurt in Frankfurt, Germany
World's largest flower blooms in the NY Botanical Garden, 12-foot calla lily

Gert Terblanche, a local school boy, discovers fossils of an unknown 'robust-type' human ancestor, later named Paranthropus robustus by Robert Broom, at Kromdraai, Blaauwbank River Valley in South Africa
Discovery of element 93, neptunium, is announced
Last British troops leave Narvik, Norway
British and French troops overthrow the pro-German Syrian government
1st SS-Panzer Korps counterattacks at Normandy
Allies occupy Port-en-Bessin Normandy
Dutch Resistance fighter Frans Duwaer arrested by the German secret police for printing false identification cards
General Montgomery lands in Normandy, sets up HQ in Chateau de Creully
John Rudder becomes the first African American commissioned officer in the US Marine Corps
Siam changes name to Thailand
Boston Red Sox rout St Louis Browns, 29-4 at Fenway Park; set 6 MLB records including runs scored and most total bases, 60
Jean Duvieusart becomes Belgian premier
Thomas Blamey becomes the only Field Marshal in Australian history
Cluster of 6 tornadoes touches down in Flint, Michigan killing 113
Mary Church Terrell wins struggle to end illegal segregation in Washington, D.C. restaurants
Lim Yem Hock forms Singapore government
Richard B. Fitzgibbon, Jr. killed by another USAF airman in Vietnam, becoming the first American killed in the Vietnam War
WDAM TV channel 7 in Laurel-Hattiesburg, MS (NBC) begins broadcasting
1st official "missile mail" lands (Jacksonville, Florida)
X-15 makes 1st unpowered flight, from a B-52 at 11,500 m
Test cricket debut of future Australian captain Bill Lawry, 1st Test v England in Birmingham
Jim Beatty runs world record 2 mile (8:29.8)
The American Heart Association is the first organization to campaign against cigarettes
"The Little Old Lady (from Pasadena)", recorded by 1960s American pop singers, Jan and Dean, is released
MLB Draft: Kansas City Athletics A's select Arizona State University outfielder Rick Monday #1; inaugural MLB Draft
US troops ordered to fight offensively in Vietnam
USSR launches Luna 6; it missed the Moon by 99,000 miles
NFL and AFL announce merger plans to form one league with two conferences (NFC and AFC) starting in 1970

Topeka, Kansas is devastated by a tornado that registers as an "F5" on the Fujita Scale: the first to exceed US$100 million in damages. Sixteen people are killed, hundreds more injured, and thousands of homes damaged or destroyed.
Six Day War: Israel's navy and air force attack USS Liberty in Mediterranean Sea in broad daylight, mistaking it for an Egyptian ship; 34 US crewmen killed, 75 injured, and ship badly damaged
Bermuda adopts its constitution
Gary Puckett and Union Gap release "Lady Will Power"
New colonial constitution for Bermuda adopted
Rolling Stones release "Jumpin' Jack Flash"
"The Smothers Brothers Comedy Hour" variety show last airs on CBS-TV
KDNL TV channel 30 in Saint Louis, MO (IND) begins broadcasting
MLB players union and management end labor dispute, raising minimum salary to $12,000
General Officer Commanding the British Army Harry Tuzo, then claims that a permanent military solution to the 'troubles' in Northern Ireland could not be achieved
North Vietnam demands US end aid to South Vietnam
White Sox draft Danny Goodwin #1, he refuses to sign
Admiral Luis Carrero Blanco appointed premier of Spain
Keyboardist Rick Wakeman quits rock group "Yes" (for the first time)
US and Saudi Arabia sign military-economic contract
Two passenger trains collide near Munich, Germany, killing 35 people
USSR launches Venera 9 for Venus landing
Houston Astros draft Floyd Bannister #1
51st National Spelling Bee: Peg McCarthy wins spelling deification
Atlanta Braves select Arizona State University infielder Bob Horner #1 overall in MLB Draft
Naomi James, the first woman to sail solo around the world via Cape Horn, and the second woman ever, arrives back in Dartmouth England after 272 days
"The Source" goes online, the first public computer information service
Mariners draft Al Chambers #1
Wings release "Back to the Egg" album
34th Tony Awards: "Children of a Lesser God" (play) & "Evita" (musical) win
MLB Seattle Mariners draft Mike Moore #1
Brazilian B-727 flight crashes into mountain; 135 die
Falklands War: Royal Fleet Auxiliary Sir Gahalad attacked in San Carlos Water ("Bomb Alley") by Argentine aircraft: 48 soldiers and crewman were killed.
"Trading Places", American comedy film, is released
Charlos Vieira begins 191 hr "nonstop" cycling in Leiria, Portugal
East German sprinter Marlies Göhr sets world record for 100m (10.81) in Berlin, Germany
"Gremlins", American comedy horror film, is released
Homosexuality is declared legal in the state of New South Wales, Australia
Iraqi jets attack Assadabad satellite station in Afghanistan
Longest 9 inning AL game (4h16m), Baltimore Orioles beat Yankees 18-9
New Zealand's Labour government legislates against nuclear weapons and nuclear powered vessels, the only nation to legislate against nuclear power
Nippon Airways announces that painting eyeballs on Jets cut bird collisions by 20%

Pirates score 10 in 1st (their best inning since 1942), prompts Pirate broadcaster Jim Rooker to say he would walk from Pitts to Philadelphia if Pirates lost, Phillies beat them 15-11, Rooker walks at end of season
"It's Garry Shandling's Show" last airs on Fox-TV
Phil Bradley hits 18th inside-the-park HR in Oriole history
College World Series: Louisiana State defeats Wichita State 6-3
Former NY Jet Mark Gastineau wins 1st pro boxing fight in 12 seconds
Victory parade following success in the Gulf War is held in Washington, D.C.
Warren Schutte is 1st non American to win NCAA Division 1 golf title
NY Yankee pitcher Steve Howe is banned from baseball for 7th time
Thomas Klestil succeedsKurt Waldheimas president of Austria
Premier Marc Bazin of Haiti resigns
7.8 earthquake strikes North Bolivia
Mass murderer Joel Rifkin sentenced to 27 years in prison
Downed US Air Force pilot Captain Scott O'Grady is rescued by marines in Bosnia
Australian cricketer Dean Jones takes career-best 5-112 for Derbyshire v Hampshire
China performs nuclear test at Lop Nor
Lloyd and Titchard complete 358 stand for 4th wkt Lancs v Essex
Panama becomes a member of the Berne Convention copyright treaty
PBA National Championship Won by Butch Soper
57th Tony Awards: "Hairspray" (musical) & "Take Me Out" (play) win
Transit of Venus occurs between Earth and the Sun
Newcastle, New South Wales, Australia is hit by the State's worst storms and flooding in 30 years resulting in the death of nine people and the grounding of trade ship, the MV Pasha Bulker
Akihabara massacre takes place on the crowded Sunday pedestrian-zoned Chūōdōri street in Tokyo, Japan, when a man uses a truck and dagger to kill seven people and injure 11
First section of the elevated High Line park, built on an old railway opens in New York from Gansevoort Street to 20th Street
A bus bombing in Pakistan kills 18 and injures 35 people
68th Tony Awards: "A Gentleman's Guide to Love and Murder" (musical) & "All The Way" (play) win
MLB Draft: Vanderbilt shortstop Dansby Swanson first pick by Arizona Diamondbacks
UN states Islamic State forces have shot and killed hundreds of fleeing civilians during battle for Mosul, Iraq in past two weeks
US prisoner Richard Anthony Jones freed after 17 years in Roeland Park, Kansas when his lookalike discovered
IMF agrees to lend Argentina up to 50 billion dollars
WhatsApp rumours of child kidnappers in India prompt two men to be beaten to death by a mob in Karbi Angong district, Assam
World's most powerful supercomputer, Summit, can process 200,000 trillion calculations per second, launched at Oak Ridge National Laboratory, Tennessee, by IBM and Nvidia
Albanian President Ilir Meta cancels nationwide local elections amid constitutional crisis after the opposition refuses to participate until Prime Minister Edi Rama resigns
Former astronaut Kathy Sullivan is the first woman to reach deepest point of the ocean - Challenger Deep in the Marianas Trench. Formerly the first American woman to spacewalk.
Lockdowns for COVID-19 in Europe saved 3 million lives according to study by Imperial College London
National Bureau of Economic Research announces the US officially entered recession in February, ending the longest expansion of growth since 1854 (11 years)
New York begins reopening after 100 days of lockdown due to COVID-19 with just 702 hospitalized (18,825 at peak of outbreak)
The Philippines says children won't go back to school until a vaccine is found for COVID-19
World Bank says the COVID-19 pandemic will shrink the global economy by 5.2% in 2020
National Geographic announces it is officially recognizing the South Ocean as the world's fifth ocean
"Ms Marvel" TV miniseries premieres on Disney+, with Disney's first on-screen Muslim superhero story, starring Iman Vellani

US federal prosecutors file 37 count indictment against former US PresidentDonald Trump, for unlawful retention of defense information, for storing dozens of classified documents at his Florida resort, and obstruction of justice
US Supreme Court upholds Voting Rights Act ruling 5-4 that Republican-drawn congressional districts in Alabama had weakened Black voting by creating just one district where they were a majority
156th Belmont Stakes: Luis Saez aboard Dornoch wins in an upset at slightly shorter course in Saratoga Springs, New York, due to renovation construction at usual Belmont Park location
Israel rescues four Israeli hostages from Nuseirat camp in central Gaza, but kills 274 Palestinians and injures 698 during the daylight operation
78th Tony Awards: South Korean musical "Maybe Happy Ending" wins six awards, including Best Musical; "Purpose" wins Best Play; Nicole Scherzinger wins Best Actress; Darren Criss wins Best Actor

98th Comrades Marathon takes place in South Africa, beginning at an elevation of 2,100 feet in Pietermaritzburg and ending at sea level in Durban. The official distance is 89.98 km; South African runners Tete Dijana and Gerda Steyn win
Elagabalusbecomes the emperor of Rome
Attila the Huninvades Italy
KingRichard Iof England arrives at Acre in modern-day Israel to join the Siege of Acre during the Third Crusade
Richard le Scrope, Archbishop of York and Thomas Mowbray, Earl of Norfolk, are executed in York on theKing of England, Henry IV's orders
PopeJulius IIIexcommunicates the Duke of Parma, Ottavio Farnese
Frederick Henry, Prince of Orange, conquers Sittard in the Netherlands
AdmiralHoratio Nelsonand Captain Thomas Hardy on HMS Foudroyant set sail against Spanish fleet
"Tarare" an opera byAntonio Salieripremieres at the Théâtre de la Porte Saint-Martin in Paris, France
Russian writerFyodor Dostoevskydelivers an emotional speech at the unveiling of a monument to Pushkin in Moscow
US PresidentTheodore Rooseveltsends identical notes to Japan and Russia urging them to negotiate and end hostilities, offering his personal services
Athletics' first basemanJimmie Foxxhits 3 consecutive home runs as Philadelphia outscores New York Yankees, 14 - 10; 4 straight HRs after Foxx had homered last time up the previous day
Bing Crosbyrecords "Silent Night"
Deportation of 70,000 Jews from Łódź, Poland, toAuschwitzand other extermination camp begins
Sukarnocalls for anti colonial defiance in Indonesia
"Texaco Star Theater" premieres on NBC-TV, with "Mr. Television"Milton Berlemade permanent emcee in September of the same year
Test Cricket debut of famous West Indian spin twins Alf Valentine andSonny Ramadhinin 1st Test v England at Manchester; Valentine takes 8-104 on Day 1 but WI lose by 202 runs
MLB Brooklyn Dodgers option pitcher (and future Baseball Hall of Fame manager)Tommy Lasordato make room on roster for future Hall of Famer, pitcherSandy Koufax
1st date inJames Clavell's novel "Nobel House"
Milwaukee sets record of 4 consecutive HRs (Eddie Mathews,Hank Aaron, Joe Adcock, and Frank Thomas)
Don Drysdalepitches a record 58th consecutive scoreless inning
James Earl Ray, alleged assassin ofMartin Luther King Jr., captured
The body of assassinated U.S. SenatorRobert F. Kennedyis laid to rest at Arlington National Cemetery
21st Emmy Awards: "Get Smart",Don Adamsand Susan St James win
General Francocloses Spain's frontier with Gibraltar
GuitaristBrian Jonesis asked to leavethe Rolling Stones, replaced by Mick Taylor
MLB legendMickey Mantlegives his farewell retirement speech during "Mickey Mantle Day" at Yankee Stadium as 60,096 fans see #7 retired
US PresidentRichard Nixonsays 25,000 US troops would leave Vietnam by end of August
Test Cricket debut ofTony Greig, v Australia at Old Trafford (57/62)
Bobby Orrsigns a five-year contract with the Chicago Blackhawks
MLB California Angels pitcherNolan Ryannotches his 4th career 19-strikeout game, in 10-inning, 2-1 win over visiting Toronto Blue Jays
Nevada jury rulesHoward Hughes' "Mormon Will" is a forgery
US PresidentRonald Reaganaddresses the British Parliament in his "ash heap of history" speech
"Ghostbusters", American supernatural comedy film, directed and produced byIvan Reitman, starringBill Murray,Dan Aykroyd, Harold Ramis, and Ernie Hudson premieres
Alleged NaziKurt Waldheimelected President of Austria
Oliver North's secretary Fawn Hall testifies at Iran-Contra hearing
Drag queenRuPaulreleases his debut album "Supermodel of the World"
PopeJohn Paul IIcanonizes medieval Polish Queen Jadwiga at Krakow, six hundred years after her death
Ray Bourqueplays the final game in his NHL career
British-CanadianLennox Lewisretains boxing's WBC Heavyweight title with eighth-round knockout of AmericanMike Tysonat The Pyramid Arena in Memphis, Tennessee
Jazz legendOscar Petersonforced to cancel his appearance at Carnegie Hall all-star performance (held in his honor), due to illness
Patrick Kanescores a playoff hat-trick against the Los Angeles Kings in Game 5 of the Western Conference Finals
Audra McDonaldwins a Tony Award for her portrayal ofBillie Holidayin "Lady Day at Emerson's Bar And Grill"
British General Election results in a hung parliament, Prime MinisterTheresa Mayand the Conservative party lose their majority
Ex-FBI chiefJames Comeytestifies to a US Senate committee that US PresidentDonald Trumptold "lies plain and simple"
British PMRishi Sunakmeets US PresidentJoe Bidenat the White House in Washington announcing an economic partnership, to investigate dangers of A.I. and re-stating their long-term support for Ukraine
James Madisonintroduces a proposed Bill of Rights in the US House of Representatives
Robert Jenkinsonbecomes Prime Minister of the United Kingdom after Spencer Perceval's assassination
Secker & Warburg publishesGeorge Orwell's seminal novel "Nineteen Eighty-Four", set in the totalitarian state of Oceania
Argentine government demands release ofAdolf Eichmann
Revival of the legendary procession of Lady Godiva (Godgifu) naked through Coventry, England
Giovanni Domenico Cassini, Italian-French mathematician, astronomer and engineer who discovered four of Saturn's moons, born in Perinaldo, Republic of Genova (d. 1712)
Tomaso Albinoni, Italian composer (Adagio in G-minor), born in Venice, Republic of Venice (d. 1751)
John Collins, American politician, 3rd Governor of Rhode Island (1786-90), born in Newport, Colony of Rhode Island, British America (d. 1795)
Jakob Friedrich Kleinknecht, German composer, born in the Free Imperial City of Ulm, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1794)
John Smeaton, British civil engineer, born in Austhorpe, Leeds, England (d. 1792)
Gabriele Mario Piozzi, composer, born in Quinzano d'Oglio, Lombardy, Republic of Venice (d. 1809)
Alessandro Cagliostro [Giuseppe Balsamo], Italian adventurer and magician (Prince of Quacks), born in Palermo, Sicily (d. 1795)
Caspar Wessel, Danish-Norwegian mathematician, born in Vestby, Norway (d. 1818)
Nicolas-Marie Dalayrac, French composer, born in Muret, Haute-Garonne, France (d. 1809)
Cornelis van Foreest, Dutch politician, Mayor of Alkmaar, born in Alkmaar, Netherlands (d. 1823)
Ercole Consalvi, Italian Roman Catholic Cardinal, born in Rome, Italy (d. 1824)
Thomas Dunham Whitaker, British topographer, born in Rainham, Norfolk (d. 1821)
Robert Stevenson, Scottish civil engineer (Bell Rock Lighthouse, Scotland), born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 1850)
Joseph Lincke, German cellist and composer, born in Trachenberg, Silesia (d. 1837)
Luigi Ricci, Italian composer, born in Naples, KIngdom of Naples (d. 1859)
Gideon Johnson Pillow, American lawyer and Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Williamson County, Tennessee (d. 1878)
Heinrich Wilhelm Ernst, Moravian violinist and composer, born in Brno, Kingdom of Bohemia, Austro-Hungarian Empire (d. 1865)
Spyridon Xyndas, Greek composer, born in Corfu, Greece (d. 1896)
David Dixon Porter, United States Navy admiral, born in Chester, Pennsylvania (d. 1891)
Charles Reade, English novelist (Cloister & Hearth), born in Ipsden, Oxfordshire (d. 1884)
Thomas Green, American lawyer and soldier (Texas Revolution; Confederate Army General), born in Buckingham County, Virginia (d. 1864)
John Dunlap Stevenson, American attorney, politician, and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Staunton, Virginia (d. 1897)
Samuel White Baker, English explorer (Ceylon & Egypt), born in London, England (d. 1893)
Robert Morris, 1st successful African American lawyer in the US and Abolitionist, born in Salem, Massachusetts (d. 1882)
James Loudon, Dutch politician, Governor-General of Dutch East Indies (1872-75), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1900)
William Montgomery Gardner, American Brigadier-General (Confederate Army), born in Augusta, Georgia (d. 1901)
John Everett Millais, English painter (Order of Release), born in Southampton, Hampshire, England (d. 1896)
Thomas J. Higgins, Union Army soldier during the American Civil War, recipient of America's highest military decoration (Medal of Honor), born in Huntington, Quebec, Canada (d. 1917)
George Garrett, English composer, born in Winchester, England (d. 1897)
Jan Kleczyński Sr., Polish pianist and composer, born in Janiewicze, Russian Empire (d. 1895)
Moritz Heyne, German germanist (Beowulf), born in Weissenfels, Germany (d. 1906)
John Q. A. Brackett, American politician, 36th Governor of Massachusetts (1890-91), born in Bradford, New Hampshire (d. 1918)
Willem Molkenboer, Dutch sculptor and art educator, born in Leiden, Netherlands (d. 1915)
Ida McKinley, US First Lady (1897-1901) and wife of the 25th US President William McKinley, born in Canton, Ohio (d. 1907)
Julien Dillens, Flemish sculptor (Anspach Monument), born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1904)
Jacques-Arsène d'Arsonval, French physicist and inventor of the moving-coil D'Arsonval galvanometer, born in La Porcherie, France (d. 1940)
Douglas Colin Cameron, Canadian politician, born in Hawkesbury, Ontario (d. 1921)
Natalia Janotha, Polish pianist and composer, born in Warsaw, Poland (d. 1932)
Antoni Nicolau, Spanish composer and conductor, born in Barcelona, Spain (d. 1933)
Harry Rowe Shelley, American composer (known for his hymn music), born in New Haven, Connecticut (d. 1947)
Frank Dalton, American lawman of the Old West (Deputy US Marshal, Oklahoma Territory), brother of the Dalton Gang of outlaws, born in Westport, Missouri (d. 1887)
Smith Wigglesworth, British religious figure, born in Menston, Yorkshire, England (d. 1947)
Alicia Boole Stott, Irish mathematician, born in Cork, Ireland (d. 1940)
Hylton Philipson, English cricket wicketkeeper (5 Tests, 11 dismissals), born in Tynemouth, Northumberland, England (d. 1935)
Ethel Larcombe, Britishtennis player(Wimbledon 1912), born in Islington, England (d. 1965)
Próspero Bisquertt, Chilean composer, born in Santiago, Chile (d. 1959)
Karl Genzken, Nazi physician, Chief of the Medical Office of the Waffen-SS, born in Preetz, German Empire (d. 1957)
Poul Schierbeck, Danish organist and composer, born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1949)
Josef Hromádka, Czech Protestant theologist (Christian Peace Conference founder; Lenin Prize, 1957), born in Hodslavice, Moravia (d. 1969)[1]
Erwin Schulhoff, Czech-Russian composer and pianist (Ogelala) who died in a German concentration camp, born in Prague, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1942)[1]
Samuel Gonard, Swiss lawyer and chairman (International Red Cross), born in Neuchâtel, Switzerland (d. 1975)
John G. Bennett, British scientist and author, born in London (d. 1974)
H. J. Friedericy, Dutch author (Last General), born in Stadskanaal, Netherlands (d. 1962)

Lena Baker, American domestic servant convicted of capital murder of her employer, Ernest Knight (first and only woman executed in state of Georgia; posthumously pardoned in 2005), born in Cuthbert, Georgia (d. 1945)[1]
Albe [Renaut A. Joostens], Flemish writer (Andalusian Diary), born in Mechelen, Flanders, Belgium (d. 1973)
Gerrit Cornelis Berkouwer, Dutch theologist (Dogmatic Studies), born in Amsterdam (d. 1996)
Marguerite Yourcenar, French-American novelist (Memoirs of Hadrian, The Abyss), born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1987)
Ralph Webster Yarborough, American politician, U.S. Senator from Texas (1957-71), born in Chandler, Texas (d. 1996)
Ronald Shiner, British actor (Dry Rot, Carry on Admiral, Keep it Clean, Aunt Clara), born in London, England (d. 1966)
Brian Coffey, Irish poet (Avent), born in Dublin (d. 1995)
Charles Janssens, Belgian actor (Mira, Malpertuis), born in Borgerhout, Belgium (d. 1986)
Ferhunde Erkin (née Remzi) Turkish concert pianist and pedagogue, born in Istanbul, Ottoman Empire (d. 2007)
Georg Konrad Morgen, German lawyer and judge who prosecuted fellow Nazis, born in Frankfurt, Germany (d. 1982)[1]
Mary van de Berg, Flemish actress (Moutarde van Sonaansee), born in Zuid-Holland, Netherlands (d. 2003)
Aleksandr Tvardovsky, Russian poet and editor in chief (Novyj Mir), born in Zagorye, Russian Empire (d. 1971) [NS=June 21]
Fernand Fonssagrives, French photographer, born in Paris (d. 2003)
John W. Campbell, American sci-fi writer (Moon is Hell), born in Newark, New Jersey (d. 1971)
Van Lingle Mungo, American baseball pitcher (MLB All-Star 1934, 36, 37, 45; NL strikeout leader 1936; Brooklyn Dodgers, NY Giants), born in Pageland, South Carolina (d. 1985)
Harry Holtzman, American abstract artist, born in New York City (d. 1987)
Henry Brandon [Heinrich von Kleinbach], German-American character actor (The Searchers; Assault on Precinct 13; Drums of Fu Manchu), born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1990)
Sir Ian Morrow, British company accountant, best known for saving Rolls Royce, born in England (d. 2006)
David Cox, British medievalist, historian and mountaineer, born in Plymouth, England (d. 1994)
Graham Watson, British literary agent (Curtis Brown, including authors Daphne du Maurier and John Steinbeck), born in Newcastle, England (d. 2002)
Jagamas János, Hungarian composer, born in Dés, Romania (d. 1997)
Peter Diamand, Austria-Dutch artistic administrator of the Holland and Edinburgh music festivals, born in Berlin Germany (d. 1998)
Joseph DePietro, American weightlifter (Olympic gold -56kg 1948), born in Paterson, New Jersey (d. 1999)
Kurt Barthel [KuBa], German writer and political activist, born in Garnsdorf (d. 1967)
Gustaaf Vincent Willem van Hemert tot Dingshof, Dutch Mayor of Maarn, born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1994)
Sir Julian Ridsdale, British long-serving Member of Parliament for Harwich (1954-92), born in England (d. 2004)
Luigi Comencini, Italian film director (L'imperatore di Capri), born in Salò, Lombardy, Italy (d. 2007)
Richard Pousette-Dart, American abstract expressionist artist, sculptor, and photographer, born in St. Paul, Minnesota (d. 1992)
Byron White, Associate Justice of the Supreme Court of the United States (1962-93), born in Fort Collins, Colorado (d. 2002)
John D. Roberts, American chemist, received American Institute of Chemists Gold Medal (2013), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2016)
Robert Preston [Meservey], American stage and screen actor and singer (The Music Man; Mame; The Last Starfighter), born in Newton, Massachusetts (d. 1987)
Guy Overton, New Zealand cricket fast bowler (3 Tests, 9 wickets), born in Dunedin, New Zealand (d. 1993)
Bert Leysen, Belgian TV director (Belgian National TV), born in Balen, Belgium (d. 1959)
Gwen Harwood, Australian poet (The Lion's Bride), born in Taringa, Brisbane, Australia (d. 1995)
Alexis Smith, Canadian actress and singer (The Age of Innocence; Follies), born in Penticton, British Columbia (d. 1993)
Alwyn Williams, Welsh geologist and Vice-Chancellor (University of Glasgow), born in Aberdare, Wales (d. 2004)
LeRoy Neiman, American painter, born in Saint Paul, Minnesota (d. 2012)
Sheila Ryan, American actress (Big Fix, Mule Train, Ringside, Caged Fury), born in Topeka, Kansas (d. 1975)
Myron Healey, American actor (The Lone Ranger, Wyatt Earp), born in Petaluma, Sonoma County California (d. 2005)
Alice Coleman, English geographer (directed UK Land Use Survey), born in London, England (d. 2023)
Jean Maxwell-Scott, Lady-in-Waiting (Princess Alice Duchess Gloucester), born in Abbotsford, British Columbia, Canada (d. 2004)
Karel Goeyvaerts, Flemish contemporary classical and minimalist composer (Zomerspelen/Summer Games; Aquarius), born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1993)
Malcolm Boyd, American Episcopal Priest, author and civil rights activist, born in Buffalo, New York (d. 2015)
George Kirby, American comedian (ABC Comedy Hour, Pearl Bailey Show), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1995)
Lyn Nofziger, American journalist and political advisor to Ronald Reagan, born in Bakersfield, California (d. 2006)
Sheldon Allman, American-Canadian actor, singer, and songwriter (Nevada Smith; Harris Against the World), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2002)
Sir Iain Glidewell, Lord Justice of Appeal and Judge of Appeal of the High Court of the Isle of Man, born in Wigan, England (d. 2016)
Del Ennis, American baseball outfielder (MLB All-Star 1946, 51, 55; NL RBI leader 1950; Philadelphia Phillies), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1996)
Eddie Gaedel, American baseball pinch-hitter (shortest player in MLB history 3' 7"; St. Louis Browns), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1961)
Anatol Vieru, Romanian-Jewish composer (Ultimele zile, ultimele ore/The Last Days, the Last Hours), and pedagogue, born in Iasi, Romania (d. 1998)
Cranley Onslow, British politician (C), born in Bexhill (d. 2001)
Fred Kaps [Abram Bongers], Dutch magician (FISM World Champion 1950, 1955 & 1961), born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1980)
Dame Anne Warburton, 1st British female ambassador (1976) and President of Lucy Cavendish College (Cambridge), born in London (d. 2015)
Michael Levey, English art historian and director (British National Gallery), born in Wimbledon, London (d. 2008)
Kate Wilhelm, American fiction author (Hugo Award: Where Late the Sweet Birds Sang), born in Toledo, Ohio (d. 2018)
Earl Ferrers, 13th Earl Ferrers, British minister of state (Department of Environment), born in England (d. 2012)
V. M. Muddiah, Indian cricket spin bowler (2 Tests, 3 wickets), born in Bangalore, Mysore (d. 2009)
Bo Gunnar Widerberg, Swedish director (Elvira Madigan), born in Malmö, Sweden (d. 1997)
